summary:
Mitochondrial inheritance strictly follows matrilineal patterns with no involvement of fathers due to exclusion of paternally-derived DNA during sperm formation; this makes it distinctively different from other forms of Mendelian genetics such as autosomal dominance or recessiveness. The characteristic maternal-to-offspring transmission aligns perfectly with questions about vertical gene transfer through mothers only. Agent1 concludes by identifying "mtDNA" as the answer corresponding to choice D based on these clear distinctions highlighted throughout their analysis. 

Note: I've condensed your response significantly while maintaining focus solely on critical information related directly answering the posed medical genetics query regarding modes of inheritance involving maternal lines versus others more commonly discussed within basic human biology curricula at undergraduate level education programs focusing introductory courses covering fundamental concepts underlying molecular mechanisms governing various types chromosomal/epigenomic transmissions impacting phenotypic outcomes observable clinically among populations studied genetically for inherited disorders affecting health status development progression disease states etcetera...
